ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Maggi Goreng Pattaya; closest parent is the generic fried noodle dish class FOODON_00002700.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Noodles, fried) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for Maggi Goreng Pattaya.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Maggi Goreng Pattaya or its immediate parent category in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Maggi Goreng Pattaya weighs approximately 350 g, comprising fried instant noodles wrapped in a thin omelette with vegetables and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Indian Fried Noodles) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Mee Goreng (Indian fried noodles) as the closest approved match. Maggi Goreng Pattaya is a variant using instant Maggi noodles wrapped in egg (Pattaya style); macronutrient profile is adapted from HPB Mee Goreng values with minor upward adjustment to fat and sodium to reflect the egg wrap and additional condiments typical of the Pattaya preparation. Values are per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Instant noodles, boiled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Maggi Goreng Pattaya. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 47–52 for cooked instant noodles (Maggi-type wheat noodles). A representative value of 49 is used. Stir-frying with oil and egg slightly lowers the effective GI relative to plain boiled noodles due to fat and protein co-ingestion. GL per serving calculated as (49 × 70.35 g available carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 34.5, rounded to 34.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1820 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Maggi Goreng Pattaya due to its very high sodium load from instant noodle seasoning, soy sauce, and chilli sauce, which can significantly exceed the recommended 2000 mg daily sodium limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 49) with high glycemic load per serving (~34.7) due to large portion of refined wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of instant noodles is moderate-to-low, the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~70 g available carbohydrate) produces a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from frying oil and egg wrap
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from stir-frying oil and the egg omelette wrap make Maggi Goreng Pattaya a food to consume occasionally and in reduced portions for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maggi Goreng Pattaya is a popular Indian-Muslim hawker dish found throughout Singapore and Malaysia, consisting of stir-fried Maggi brand instant wheat noodles that are enveloped in a thin folded omelette — the defining 'Pattaya' presentation. The noodles are wok-fried at high heat with egg, onion, tomato, green chilli, cabbage, bean sprouts, and tofu, seasoned with soy sauce, chilli sauce, and tomato sauce. The finished dish is plated with the omelette folded over the noodles like an envelope, often garnished with sliced chilli and lime. The texture combines the soft chewiness of the noodles with the silky egg wrap and the crunch of bean sprouts and cabbage. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet from the tomato and chilli sauces. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g hawker serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 20 g protein, 18 g fat, and 70 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1820 mg per serving) due to instant noodle seasoning and condiments, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index of the instant noodle base is approximately 49 (low-moderate), but the high carbohydrate load per serving produces a high glycemic load (~34.7), which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is dairy-free but contains gluten and egg allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Mee Goreng (Indian fried noodles) — the closest approved primary source — with minor adjustments for the egg wrap and additional condiments of the Pattaya preparation. No exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry for Maggi Goreng Pattaya exists. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for instant noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Micronutrients not reported by HPB Foodo for this dish category are set to -1 (sentinel value for not reported in source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all -1 as they are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. GI of 49 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for instant noodles (Maggi-type); no direct GI measurement for Maggi Goreng Pattaya was found in the approved literature. GL per serving = (49 × 70.35) / 100 ≈ 34.5, reported as 34.7 after rounding. The 'Pattaya' style refers to the omelette-wrapped presentation and is not a geographic reference to Pattaya, Thailand. Halal status reflects typical mamak/Indian-Muslim hawker preparation; individual restaurant compliance should be verified. Cuisine tag 'Drinks & Beverages' from the field survey source record is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded.
